title: Number of people living in extreme poverty
subtitle: Extreme poverty is defined as living below the [International Poverty Line](#dod:international-poverty-line) of $3 per day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Number of people living in extreme poverty, World, 1990 to 2026

Extreme poverty is defined as living below the International Poverty Line of $3 per day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This area chart tracks the global population distribution between those living in extreme poverty and those not in extreme poverty from 1990 to 2026. The vertical axis measures the number of people in billions, ranging from 0 to 9 billion, while the horizontal axis marks time in five-year intervals. The data is expressed in international-$ at 2021 prices and relates to income or consumption per capita. Two distinct colored regions represent the population segments: a lower dark pink area labeled "Living in extreme poverty" and an upper light orange area labeled "Not in extreme poverty." In 1990, the population living in extreme poverty stands just above 2 billion, while the population not in extreme poverty begins at approximately 3.3 billion. Over the subsequent decades, the dark pink area shows a consistent downward trajectory, falling below 2 billion around 2005 and dropping below 1 billion by 2015. By 2026, the number of people living in extreme poverty reaches its lowest point on the chart, sitting well below the 1 billion mark. Conversely, the light orange area expands steadily throughout the timeline, rising from roughly 3.3 billion in 1990 to over 8 billion in 2026. The total height of the stacked areas increases from roughly 5.3 billion to over 8.3 billion, showing global population growth alongside the shift in poverty distribution. The visual gap between the two categories widens significantly as time progresses, with the orange section occupying the vast majority of the chart area by the final year. The data shows a clear divergence where the population not in extreme poverty grows in absolute numbers while the population in extreme poverty shrinks.
